name: Fivetran
what_it_does: Fully managed data pipeline service that automates data movement from 500+ sources to data warehouses with zero-maintenance connectors, offering a REST API for connector and sync management automation.
use_cases: Triggering on-demand syncs for specific connectors via API for time-sensitive workflows
Monitoring connector health and sync status in automated data pipeline dashboards
Programmatically creating and configuring connectors for multi-tenant data architectures
Pausing and resuming connectors based on business logic or cost management rules
Building data pipeline observability into agent-driven operations
not_for: Custom transformation logic (Fivetran handles ingestion only; pair with dbt for transforms)
Real-time streaming data — Fivetran syncs are scheduled batch (minimum 1-minute intervals on some connectors)
Teams wanting open-source control over their pipeline code (use Airbyte OSS)
Very budget-constrained teams — Fivetran is premium-priced
best_when: You need zero-maintenance, enterprise-grade managed connectors and your team doesn't want to build or maintain ETL code, and can justify the premium pricing.
avoid_when: Budget is a primary concern, you need custom connector development, or you want open-source control over your data pipelines.

auth.notes: API key and secret pair with Base64-encoded Basic Auth. Keys are account-scoped. No fine-grained per-resource permission scoping currently available.

pricing.paid_tiers: Pricing based on monthly active rows (MAR) synced; Starter, Standard, Enterprise tiers
pricing.notes: Premium pricing — often 3-5x more expensive than Airbyte Cloud for similar volume. No free tier; 14-day trial available. Enterprise discount possible at scale.

performance.rate_limits: API rate limits not prominently documented; standard limits apply

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: API returns standard HTTP codes with JSON error details. Connector failure reasons are surfaced with descriptive messages helpful for automated diagnosis.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Connector sync triggers are not idempotent — triggering twice may queue two syncs. Connector configuration updates are idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: Connector IDs are system-generated strings — store them after creation for future API calls
Manual sync triggers don't override scheduled syncs — understand the sync schedule interaction
Connector status has many states (syncing, paused, broken, etc.) — agents must handle all states gracefully
Schema changes can break syncs — monitor for schema change alerts in automated pipelines
High-frequency API polling for sync status has rate limit implications — use webhooks instead
